1	{
2	  "feature": "Verbum Implementation",
3	  "description": "# Catholic Content Management Platform – PRD

### TL;DR

A personal platform for Ramir to manage the full lifecycle of Catholic content — from AI-assisted generation (Claude, ChatGPT) to theological validation via NotebookLM, editorial scheduling, and multi-channel delivery. It specifically supports short-form video scripts (1-min), long-form video scripts (10-min), and SEO-optimized blog posts in Portuguese, ensuring both speed and doctrinal accuracy for a Catholic content creator.

---

## Goals

### Business Goals

* Centralize all Catholic content production activities in a single platform.
* Reduce average time-to-publish for each piece of content by at least 50%.
* Increase consistency in publishing, achieving a minimum of 3 content pieces per week.
* Ensure 100% of published content is validated for theological accuracy.
* Minimize post-publication corrections by implementing robust doctrinal review.

### User Goals

* Rapidly generate content using AI while maintaining a uniquely Catholic voice.
* Validate content against authoritative Church sources (without building custom RAG pipelines).
* Organize content into an accessible editorial calendar with clear status tracking.
* Schedule delivery for each piece on appropriate channels (e.g., YouTube, Blog).
* Easily curate, update, and archive content with full version and validation history.

### Non-Goals

* Building or maintaining a custom Catholic knowledge base (handled entirely by NotebookLM).
* Undertaking broad social media management (e.g., analytics, community interaction) beyond basic scheduling/delivery reminders.
* Implementing monetization or revenue-generating features.

---

## User Stories

**Persona:** Ramir, Catholic Content Creator

* As Ramir, I want to create a new short-form video script using Claude or ChatGPT, so that I can generate engaging 1-min video content quickly.
* As Ramir, I want to validate my script against the full Catholic corpus available in NotebookLM, so that I am confident my content aligns with Church teaching.
* As Ramir, I want to schedule my validated content on an editorial calendar, so that I maintain a consistent publishing cadence across channels.
* As Ramir, I want to curate and edit blog posts for SEO (in Portuguese), so that my written content reaches a larger audience via search engines.
* As Ramir, I want to review the current status (draft, validated, scheduled, published) and destination (YouTube, Blog, etc.) of all my content, so that I’m always aware of what’s next.

---

## Functional Requirements

* **Content Creation (Priority: High)**

  * AI Drafting Integration: Connect to Claude and ChatGPT APIs for rapid content generation.
  * Content Type Templates: Pre-configured templates for short-form video, long-form video, and blog posts.

* **Theological Validation (Priority: High)**

  * NotebookLM Integration: Use a third-party library for doctrinal source validation.
  * Validation Score/Flag System: Visual feedback indicating theological alignment.
  * Source Citation Display: Display relevant citations from the Catholic corpus.

* **Editorial Calendar (Priority: Medium)**

  * Scheduling by Content Type and Channel: Assign publication dates and platforms.
  * Status Tracking: Transparently manage content states (draft, validated, scheduled, published).

* **SEO & Curation Tools (Priority: Medium)**

  * Portuguese SEO Metadata: Fields and checks for keywords, meta, and descriptions in Portuguese.
  * Readability Checks: Automated assessment of text clarity.
  * Keyword Suggestions: AI-powered prompts for blog post optimization.

* **Channel Management (Priority: Low)**

  * Channel Tagging: Associate content with YouTube, Blog, or other platforms.
  * Delivery Reminders: Notify when content is due to be published or reviewed.

---

## User Experience

**Entry Point & First-Time User Experience**

* User logs in and lands on a dashboard summarizing the content pipeline, showing draft/validated/scheduled/published items, and the editorial calendar.
* Optional onboarding walkthrough highlights how to create content, trigger validation, and use the editor.

**Core Experience**

* **Step 1:** Create a new content item.
  * Choose content type: short video, long video, or blog post.
  * Select language preference (default: Portuguese for blog).
  * Clear, minimal form – no unnecessary friction.
* **Step 2:** AI generation panel.
  * Choose AI model: Claude or ChatGPT.
  * Enter topic/prompt and generate draft.
  * Validate prompt entry; handle API errors gracefully.
  * Show generated draft in main editor.
* **Step 3:** Trigger NotebookLM validation.
  * One-click validation sends content to third-party NotebookLM library.
  * Return and display validation score/flags and doctrinal citations.
  * Validation panel anchored on the right side of the editor.
* **Step 4:** Content curation and editing.
  * Edit draft based on validation results.
  * Option to view citation sources directly.
  * Save drafts and track validation history.
* **Step 5:** Assign to channel and schedule.
  * Tag content to delivery platform.
  * Choose publication date via editorial calendar UI.
  * Status updates to “Scheduled.”
* **Step 6:** Mark as published and archive.
  * Mark content as published after delivery.
  * Optionally archive for future reference and analytics.

**Advanced Features & Edge Cases**

* Bulk scheduling: Assign dates and channels for multiple items at once.
* Validation history: View prior validation runs and responses.
* Content versioning: Track major edits and revert if necessary.
* Handle validation or AI API downtime gracefully (clear errors, retry options).

**UI/UX Highlights**

* Single-column editor central to workflow; validation feedback panel fixed to the right.
* Editorial calendar as a visual week/month view; click-in to schedule or review items.
* Portuguese-first interface and localization for SEO/blog workflows.
* Consistent, accessible color palette and responsive design for all device types.

---

## Narrative

Ramir sits down at his desk on a Tuesday morning with inspiration to create a 1-minute video script about the upcoming Feast of Corpus Christi. He opens his Catholic Content Management Platform and immediately sees his dashboard, which highlights content drafts and this week’s publishing schedule. Selecting the “short video” template, he enters a brief prompt for Claude, who quickly generates a concise, engaging script in less than a minute.

Before moving forward, Ramir clicks the “Validate with NotebookLM” button. Within seconds, the platform provides a score indicating strong doctrinal support but flags two statements for clarification. The tool seamlessly surfaces source citations from the Catechism and Vatican II documents, making it easy for Ramir to reference and refine his wording to ensure precise Catholic teaching.

Satisfied with the script, he assigns it to his short-video channel and schedules it for Thursday’s publication. Motivated by the smooth workflow, he turns to the blog post section—writes, validates, optimizes SEO in Portuguese, and schedules another article, all in one productive sitting. Ramir knows that every piece he publishes aligns with Church authority, publishes on time, and is organized for maximum reach—giving him both confidence and creative momentum.

---

## Success Metrics

### User-Centric Metrics

* Average time (in minutes) from content idea to scheduled item (tracked via timestamps).
* Number of validation cycles per content piece before approval.
* Number of content pieces published per week.
* User feedback on validation accuracy and workflow satisfaction.

### Business Metrics

* Consistency of weekly publishing cadence (minimum 3 items/week).
* Reduction in after-publication corrections or removals due to doctrinal issues.
* Adoption/continued use rate (e.g., items managed per week/month).

### Technical Metrics

* Average response time from NotebookLM API (<2 seconds per call).
* Average AI generation latency (<5 seconds per request).
* Content editor uptime (>99% during weekday business hours).
* Robust error-rate tracking on API integrations (<2% failure rate).

### Tracking Plan

* Content item created (event)
* AI generation triggered (event, with selected model)
* Validation initiated/completed (event, with score/result)
* Content scheduled (event, with channel/date)
* Marked as published (event)
* User edits/re-validation cycles (event)

---

## Technical Considerations

### Technical Needs

* Web application frontend with content editor, dashboard, and calendar views.
* API integrations for Anthropic (Claude), OpenAI (ChatGPT), and third-party NotebookLM library.
* Lightweight backend for routing requests, managing validation/scheduling logic, and storing user data.
* Database for persistent storage of content items, validation responses, scheduling metadata, and channel assignments.

### Integration Points

* Anthropic Claude API for content drafting.
* OpenAI ChatGPT API for alternate drafting option.
* NotebookLM third-party library for source validation and citation retrieval.
* Option for editorial calendar export (iCal or Google Calendar integration for reminders).

### Data Storage & Privacy

* All data (drafts, validation results, scheduling info) stored privately and securely for Ramir only.
* Ensure encryption at rest and in transit.
* No personal data shared externally or sold.
* Compliance with GDPR and local privacy standards as relevant.

### Scalability & Performance

* Designed for single-user, low daily volume (tens of content items per week).
* Minimal scaling risk.
* Prioritize fast response for workflow efficiency.

### Potential Challenges

* Reliability and rate limits of NotebookLM’s third-party library/API.
* Maintaining consistent, high-quality Portuguese output from AI models (especially for SEO).
* Managing failing API calls or downtime from AI or validation services.\

entries.content:
Now I have a thorough understanding of the task and the project state. Let me create the complexity assessment.

**Key observations:**
1. **Greenfield project** — the repo has only a prototypes-magic directory with a basic Vite/React template; no backend, no database, no services
2. **3 external integrations** — Anthropic Claude API, OpenAI ChatGPT API, NotebookLM third-party library
3. **Full application** — dashboard, editor, calendar, validation panel, content lifecycle management
4. **Database needed** — content items, validation history, scheduling metadata (from scratch)
5. **Infrastructure setup** — backend server, database, environment config, API key management
6. **No existing patterns** — empty project_index.json; no conventions, no services defined

entries.detail: File created successfully at: /home/rlmit/workspace/musicalizer/.auto-claude/specs/002-verbum-implementation/complexity_assessment.json

entries.content:
The complexity assessment is complete. Here's a summary of my findings:

## Complexity Assessment: **COMPLEX** (confidence: 0.90)

### Key Findings

| Dimension | Assessment | Details |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | ~40 files, 3 services | Full-stack greenfield: frontend, backend, database — all from scratch |
| **Integrations** | 3 external APIs | Claude, ChatGPT, NotebookLM — all require API key management and error handling |
| **Infrastructure** | Database + config | Schema design from scratch, 3+ API keys, environment setup |
| **Knowledge** | No existing patterns | Empty project_index; only a bare Vite/React prototype shell exists |
| **Risk** | Medium | NotebookLM reliability (PRD-flagged), API key security, Portuguese AI quality |

### Why COMPLEX (not STANDARD)
1. **3 external integrations** — crosses the 2+ threshold immediately
2. **Database from scratch** — no existing schema, need full data model design
3. **No codebase patterns** — greenfield means every architectural decision is net-new
4. **NotebookLM is unfamiliar tech** — needs dedicated research to identify the right library and understand its API surface

### Recommended Pipeline
Full 8-phase pipeline: `discovery → requirements → research → context → spec_writing → self_critique → planning → validation`

### Validation: HIGH risk
- Unit + Integration + E2E tests required
- Security scan required (API key handling, encryption requirements, GDPR)
- No staging deployment needed (single-user personal tool)

1	"""Application configuration and environment variable loading."""
2	
3	from __future__ import annotations
4	
5	import os
6	from dataclasses import dataclass
7	from pathlib import Path
8	
9	from dotenv import load_dotenv
10	
11	
12	class ConfigurationError(Exception):
13	    """Raised when required configuration is missing or invalid."""
14	
15	
16	@dataclass(frozen=True)
17	class Settings:
18	    """Application settings loaded from environment variables."""
19	
20	    gemini_api_key: str | None
21	    chutes_api_key: str | None
22	
23	    def __init__(self) -> None:
24	        # Load .env from project root (walk up from this file)
25	        env_path = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent.parent / ".env"
26	        load_dotenv(env_path)
27	
28	        object.__setattr__(self, "gemini_api_key", os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"))
29	        object.__setattr__(self, "chutes_api_key", os.getenv("CHUTES_API_KEY"))
30	
31	    def require_gemini_key(self) -> str:
32	        """Return the Gemini API key or raise ConfigurationError."""
33	        if not self.gemini_api_key:
34	            raise ConfigurationError(
35	                "GEMINI_API_KEY is not set. "
36	                "Add it to your .env file or export it as an environment variable.\n"
37	                "  export GEMINI_API_KEY=your-key-here"
38	            )
39	        return self.gemini_api_key
40	
41	    def require_chutes_key(self) -> str:
42	        """Return the Chutes API key or raise ConfigurationError."""
43	        if not self.chutes_api_key:
44	            raise ConfigurationError(
45	                "CHUTES_API_KEY is not set. "
46	                "Add it to your .env file or export it as an environment variable.\n"
47	                "  export CHUTES_API_KEY=your-key-here"
48	            )
49	        return self.chutes_api_key

1	"""Pydantic data models for Musicalizer."""
2	
3	from enum import Enum
4	
5	from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
6	
7	
8	class Verdict(str, Enum):
9	    GOOD = "good"
10	    BAD = "bad"
11	
12	
13	class SunoOutput(BaseModel):
14	    """Suno-optimized song output."""
15	
16	    style_prompt: str = Field(description="Suno style prompt: genre, instrumentation, mood")
17	    lyrics_prompt: str = Field(description="Suno lyrics with structural tags like [Verse], [Chorus]")
18	    title: str = Field(description="Song title")
19	
20	
21	class EvalResult(BaseModel):
22	    """Structured evaluation of a generation."""
23	
24	    verdict: Verdict
25	    score: float = Field(ge=0.0, le=1.0, description="Quality score 0-1")
26	    rationale: str = Field(description="Why this verdict was given")
27	    strengths: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
28	    weaknesses: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
29	    improvements: list[str] = Field(description="Specific actionable improvements")
30	
31	
32	class Preset(BaseModel):
33	    """Generation preset configuration."""
34	
35	    name: str = Field(description="Preset identifier (e.g., 'brazilian-catholic')")
36	    description: str = Field(description="Human-readable preset description")
37	    language: str = Field(description="Target language code (e.g., 'pt-BR')")
38	    style: str = Field(description="Full style_prompt text for Suno")
39	    genre: str = Field(description="Primary genre (e.g., 'Catholic hymn')")
40	    mood: str = Field(description="Emotional mood (e.g., 'devotional')")
41	    tempo: str = Field(default="moderate", description="Tempo feel")
42	    lyric_themes: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list, description="Thematic keywords for lyrics generation")
43	    structural_tags: list[str] = Field(
44	        default_factory=lambda: ["[Verse]", "[Chorus]", "[Bridge]", "[Outro]"],
45	        description="Suno structural tags to use in lyrics_prompt",
46	    )
47	
48	
49	class GenerationRecord(BaseModel):
50	    """Record of a single generation iteration in the auto-improvement loop."""
51	
52	    iteration: int = Field(description="Iteration number (1-based)")
53	    output: SunoOutput = Field(description="Generated song output")
54	    evaluation: EvalResult | None = Field(default=None, description="Evaluation result")
55	    parent_iteration: int | None = Field(default=None, description="Parent iteration for lineage tracking")
56	    timestamp: str = Field(description="ISO timestamp of generation")

1	from google import genai
2	from google.genai import types
3	from pydantic import BaseModel
4	
5	from musicalizer.providers.base import LLMProvider
6	
7	
8	class GeminiProvider(LLMProvider):
9	    """Google Gemini LLM provider using google-genai client."""
10	
11	    def __init__(self, api_key: str, model: str = "gemini-2.5-flash") -> None:
12	        self.client = genai.Client(api_key=api_key)
13	        self.model = model
14	
15	    async def generate(
16	        self,
17	        system_prompt: str,
18	        user_prompt: str,
19	        response_schema: type[BaseModel] | None = None,
20	    ) -> str:
21	        """Generate a completion from Gemini."""
22	        config_kwargs: dict = {
23	            "system_instruction": system_prompt,
24	        }
25	        if response_schema is not None:
26	            config_kwargs["response_json_schema"] = response_schema.model_json_schema()
27	            config_kwargs["response_mime_type"] = "application/json"
28	
29	        config = types.GenerateContentConfig(**config_kwargs)
30	        response = await self.client.aio.models.generate_content(
31	            model=self.model,
32	            contents=user_prompt,
33	            config=config,
34	        )
35	        return response.text
36	
37	    def get_model_name(self) -> str:
38	        """Return the model identifier."""
39	        return self.model

1	import json
2	
3	from openai import AsyncOpenAI
4	from pydantic import BaseModel
5	
6	from musicalizer.providers.base import LLMProvider
7	
8	
9	class ChutesProvider(LLMProvider):
10	    """Chutes LLM provider using OpenAI-compatible API."""
11	
12	    def __init__(self, api_key: str, model: str = "deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-R1") -> None:
13	        self.client = AsyncOpenAI(
14	            api_key=api_key,
15	            base_url="https://llm.chutes.ai/v1/",
16	        )
17	        self.model = model
18	
19	    async def generate(
20	        self,
21	        system_prompt: str,
22	        user_prompt: str,
23	        response_schema: type[BaseModel] | None = None,
24	    ) -> str:
25	        """Generate a completion from Chutes API."""
26	        messages: list[dict[str, str]] = [
27	            {"role": "system", "content": system_prompt},
28	            {"role": "user", "content": user_prompt},
29	        ]
30	        kwargs: dict = {}
31	        if response_schema is not None:
32	            kwargs["response_format"] = {
33	                "type": "json_schema",
34	                "json_schema": {
35	                    "name": "response",
36	                    "schema": response_schema.model_json_schema(),
37	                },
38	            }
39	            try:
40	                response = await self.client.chat.completions.create(
41	                    model=self.model,
42	                    messages=messages,
43	                    **kwargs,
44	                )
45	                return response.choices[0].message.content
46	            except Exception:
47	                # Fall back to json_object mode with schema in system prompt.
48	                kwargs["response_format"] = {"type": "json_object"}
49	                schema_str = json.dumps(response_schema.model_json_schema())
50	                messages[0]["content"] += (
51	                    f"\n\nRespond with valid JSON matching this schema:\n"
52	                    f"{schema_str}"
53	                )
54	
55	        response = await self.client.chat.completions.create(
56	            model=self.model,
57	            messages=messages,
58	            **kwargs,
59	        )
60	        return response.choices[0].message.content
61	
62	    def get_model_name(self) -> str:
63	        """Return the model identifier."""
64	        return self.model
65

1	"""Song generation engine — orchestrates provider + preset → SunoOutput."""
2	
3	from __future__ import annotations
4	
5	import json
6	import logging
7	
8	from pydantic import ValidationError
9	
10	from musicalizer.models import EvalResult, Preset, SunoOutput
11	from musicalizer.prompts import build_generation_prompt
12	from musicalizer.providers.base import LLMProvider
13	
14	logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
15	
16	MAX_RETRIES = 2
17	
18	
19	class GenerationError(Exception):
20	    """Raised when song generation fails after retries."""
21	
22	
23	async def generate_song(
24	    provider: LLMProvider,
25	    preset: Preset,
26	    prior_feedback: EvalResult | None = None,
27	) -> SunoOutput:
28	    """Generate a Suno-optimized song using the given provider and preset.
29	
30	    If *prior_feedback* is supplied, the prompt incorporates evaluation
31	    feedback so the LLM can produce an improved version.
32	
33	    Implements retry logic for malformed responses (Edge Case 3): on the
34	    first failure the prompt is tightened and the request is retried once.
35	    If still invalid, a ``GenerationError`` is raised.
36	    """
37	    system_prompt, user_prompt = build_generation_prompt(preset, prior_feedback)
38	
39	    last_error: Exception | None = None
40	    raw_response: str | None = None
41	
42	    for attempt in range(1, MAX_RETRIES + 1):
43	        try:
44	            raw_response = await provider.generate(
45	                system_prompt=system_prompt,
46	                user_prompt=user_prompt,
47	                response_schema=SunoOutput,
48	            )
49	            return _parse_response(raw_response)
50	        except (ValidationError, json.JSONDecodeError, KeyError, TypeError) as exc:
51	            last_error = exc
52	            logger.warning(
53	                "Attempt %d/%d: malformed response from %s — %s",
54	                attempt,
55	                MAX_RETRIES,
56	                provider.get_model_name(),
57	                exc,
58	            )
59	            # Tighten prompt for retry
60	            system_prompt, user_prompt = _stricter_prompt(system_prompt, user_prompt)
61	
62	    # All retries exhausted
63	    logger.error(
64	        "Generation failed after %d attempts. Last raw response: %s",
65	        MAX_RETRIES,
66	        raw_response,
67	    )
68	    raise GenerationError(
69	        f"Failed to generate valid SunoOutput after {MAX_RETRIES} attempts: {last_error}"
70	    )
71	
72	
73	def _parse_response(raw: str) -> SunoOutput:
74	    """Parse a raw LLM response string into a validated ``SunoOutput``."""
75	    # If the provider already returned valid JSON, parse directly.
76	    data = json.loads(raw) if isinstance(raw, str) else raw
77	    return SunoOutput.model_validate(data)
78	
79	
80	def _stricter_prompt(system_prompt: str, user_prompt: str) -> tuple[str, str]:
81	    """Return tightened prompts that emphasise valid JSON output."""
82	    strict_suffix = (
83	        "\n\nCRITICAL: Your response MUST be valid JSON with exactly these "
84	        'keys: "style_prompt" (string), "lyrics_prompt" (string), "title" '
85	        "(string). Do NOT include any other text outside the JSON object."
86	    )
87	    return system_prompt + strict_suffix, user_prompt
88

# Musicalizer

AI Music Generation, Evaluation & Auto-Improvement Pipeline. Generate Suno-optimized songs using LLM providers (Gemini, Chutes), evaluate quality, and iteratively improve output through an automated feedback loop.

## Installation

```bash
pip install -e ".[dev]"
```

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Description |
|---|---|
| `GEMINI_API_KEY` | Google Gemini API key |
| `CHUTES_API_KEY` | Chutes AI API key |

At least one API key must be set. Create a `.env` file in the project root or export directly.

## Usage

### Generate a song

```bash
musicalizer generate --preset brazilian-catholic --provider gemini
```

### Evaluate a generated song

```bash
musicalizer evaluate --input output/generate_brazilian-catholic_20240101T120000.json
```

### Auto-improve (generate → evaluate → reflect → regenerate)

```bash
musicalizer auto-improve --preset brazilian-catholic --provider gemini --iterations 5 --target-score 0.8
```

### List available presets

```bash
musicalizer presets list
```

## Presets

| Preset | Description |
|---|---|
| `brazilian-catholic` | Brazilian Catholic worship music |
| `brazilian-catholic-christmas` | Brazilian Catholic Christmas music |
| `brazilian-catholic-marian` | Brazilian Catholic Marian devotional music |

## Development

```bash
pip install -e ".[dev]"
pytest tests/ -v
```

[build-system]
requires = ["setuptools>=68.0", "setuptools-scm>=8.0"]
build-backend = "setuptools.build_meta"

[project]
name = "musicalizer"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "AI Music Generation, Evaluation & Auto-Improvement Pipeline"
requires-python = ">=3.11"
dependencies = [
    "google-genai[aiohttp]>=1.68.0",
    "openai>=1.0.0",
    "pydantic>=2.0.0",
    "click>=8.0.0",
    "python-dotenv>=1.0.0",
    "rich>=13.0.0",
]

[project.optional-dependencies]
dev = [
    "pytest>=7.0.0",
    "pytest-asyncio>=0.21.0",
    "pytest-mock>=3.0.0",
]

[project.scripts]
musicalizer = "musicalizer.cli:cli"

[tool.setuptools.packages.find]
where = ["src"]

[tool.pytest.ini_options]
testpaths = ["tests"]
asyncio_mode = "auto"

11	def create_provider(provider_name: str, api_key: str) -> LLMProvider:
12	    """Create an LLM provider instance by name.
13	
14	    Args:
15	        provider_name: Name of the provider (e.g. "chutes", "gemini").
16	        api_key: API key for the provider.
17	
18	    Returns:
19	        An instance of the requested LLM provider.
20	
21	    Raises:
22	        ValueError: If the provider name is not recognized.
23	    """
24	    provider_cls = _PROVIDERS.get(provider_name)
25	    if provider_cls is None:
26	        available = ", ".join(sorted(_PROVIDERS))
27	        raise ValueError(
28	            f"Unknown provider '{provider_name}'. Available providers: {available}"
29	        )
30	    return provider_cls(api_key=api_key)

entries.content:
The research.json is valid. Let me print the summary:

```
=== RESEARCH COMPLETE ===

Integrations Researched: 6
- Anthropic Claude API (Python SDK): Verified ✓
  Package: anthropic >=0.88.0 | pip install anthropic
  
- OpenAI ChatGPT API (Python SDK): Verified ✓
  Package: openai >=2.30.0 | pip install openai
  
- NotebookLM Integration (notebooklm-py): Verified ⚠ HIGH RISK
  Package: notebooklm-py >=0.3.4 | UNOFFICIAL - uses undocumented Google APIs
  
- FastAPI (Backend Framework): Verified ✓
  Package: fastapi >=0.135.0 | pip install "fastapi[standard]"
  
- SQLModel (Database ORM): Verified ✓
  Package: sqlmodel >=0.0.37 | pip install sqlmodel + aiosqlite
  
- icalendar (Calendar Export): Verified ✓
  Package: icalendar | pip install icalendar

Unverified Claims: 5
- NotebookLM <2s response time: MEDIUM risk (no SLA, undocumented APIs)
- NotebookLM long-term stability: HIGH risk (can break without notice)
- NotebookLM handles full Catholic corpus: MEDIUM risk (requires manual source upload, has limits)
- AI generation <5s: LOW risk (streaming mitigates perceived latency)
- Portuguese SEO quality: LOW risk (both models support PT, needs prompt tuning)

Key Findings:
1. ⚠ CRITICAL: NotebookLM has NO stable official API for consumer use. The notebooklm-py library is unofficial, community-maintained, and uses undocumented Google APIs that can break at any time. The official Enterprise API requires paid Google Workspace Enterprise licensing (overkill for single-user). This is the #1 project risk.

2. NotebookLM's chat.ask() API returns answers with inline citations and ChatReference objects (source_id, cited_text, citation_number) - exactly what's needed for theological validation with source citations.

3. notebooklm-py requires browser-based Google authentication (Playwright + Chromium ~200MB) - not a simple API key. Auth tokens stored at ~/.notebooklm/ and auto-refresh.

4. All AI SDKs (Anthropic, OpenAI) are mature, well-documented, with native async and streaming support - low risk.

5. Python 3.10+ required (FastAPI >=0.130.0 dropped 3.9 support). Existing project uses 3.11+ ✓

6. Existing React/Vite/TypeScript/Tailwind frontend can be extended - no new frontend framework needed.

Recommendations:
1. CRITICAL: Build NotebookLM behind an abstraction layer with fallback to Claude/GPT-based validation
2. Use async throughout: AsyncAnthropic, AsyncOpenAI, async FastAPI, async SQLModel
3. SQLite + SQLModel is ideal for single-user - no DB server needed
4. Implement streaming SSE for AI generation to improve perceived performance
5. Use Chat Completions API (not Responses API) for OpenAI stability

research.json created successfully at:
.auto-claude/specs/002-verbum-implementation/research.json
```

1	{
2	  "task_description": "# Catholic Content Management Platform – PRD

### TL;DR

A personal platform for Ramir to manage the full lifecycle of Catholic content — from AI-assisted generation (Claude, ChatGPT) to theological validation via NotebookLM, editorial scheduling, and multi-channel delivery. It specifically supports short-form video scripts (1-min), long-form video scripts (10-min), and SEO-optimized blog posts in Portuguese, ensuring both speed and doctrinal accuracy for a Catholic content creator.

---

## Goals

### Business Goals

* Centralize all Catholic content production activities in a single platform.
* Reduce average time-to-publish for each piece of content by at least 50%.
* Increase consistency in publishing, achieving a minimum of 3 content pieces per week.
* Ensure 100% of published content is validated for theological accuracy.
* Minimize post-publication corrections by implementing robust doctrinal review.

### User Goals

* Rapidly generate content using AI while maintaining a uniquely Catholic voice.
* Validate content against authoritative Church sources (without building custom RAG pipelines).
* Organize content into an accessible editorial calendar with clear status tracking.
* Schedule delivery for each piece on appropriate channels (e.g., YouTube, Blog).
* Easily curate, update, and archive content with full version and validation history.

### Non-Goals

* Building or maintaining a custom Catholic knowledge base (handled entirely by NotebookLM).
* Undertaking broad social media management (e.g., analytics, community interaction) beyond basic scheduling/delivery reminders.
* Implementing monetization or revenue-generating features.

---

## User Stories

**Persona:** Ramir, Catholic Content Creator

* As Ramir, I want to create a new short-form video script using Claude or ChatGPT, so that I can generate engaging 1-min video content quickly.
* As Ramir, I want to validate my script against the full Catholic corpus available in NotebookLM, so that I am confident my content aligns with Church teaching.
* As Ramir, I want to schedule my validated content on an editorial calendar, so that I maintain a consistent publishing cadence across channels.
* As Ramir, I want to curate and edit blog posts for SEO (in Portuguese), so that my written content reaches a larger audience via search engines.
* As Ramir, I want to review the current status (draft, validated, scheduled, published) and destination (YouTube, Blog, etc.) of all my content, so that I’m always aware of what’s next.

---

## Functional Requirements

* **Content Creation (Priority: High)**

  * AI Drafting Integration: Connect to Claude and ChatGPT APIs for rapid content generation.
  * Content Type Templates: Pre-configured templates for short-form video, long-form video, and blog posts.

* **Theological Validation (Priority: High)**

  * NotebookLM Integration: Use a third-party library for doctrinal source validation.
  * Validation Score/Flag System: Visual feedback indicating theological alignment.
  * Source Citation Display: Display relevant citations from the Catholic corpus.

* **Editorial Calendar (Priority: Medium)**

  * Scheduling by Content Type and Channel: Assign publication dates and platforms.
  * Status Tracking: Transparently manage content states (draft, validated, scheduled, published).

* **SEO & Curation Tools (Priority: Medium)**

  * Portuguese SEO Metadata: Fields and checks for keywords, meta, and descriptions in Portuguese.
  * Readability Checks: Automated assessment of text clarity.
  * Keyword Suggestions: AI-powered prompts for blog post optimization.

* **Channel Management (Priority: Low)**

  * Channel Tagging: Associate content with YouTube, Blog, or other platforms.
  * Delivery Reminders: Notify when content is due to be published or reviewed.

---

## User Experience

**Entry Point & First-Time User Experience**

* User logs in and lands on a dashboard summarizing the content pipeline, showing draft/validated/scheduled/published items, and the editorial calendar.
* Optional onboarding walkthrough highlights how to create content, trigger validation, and use the editor.

**Core Experience**

* **Step 1:** Create a new content item.
  * Choose content type: short video, long video, or blog post.
  * Select language preference (default: Portuguese for blog).
  * Clear, minimal form – no unnecessary friction.
* **Step 2:** AI generation panel.
  * Choose AI model: Claude or ChatGPT.
  * Enter topic/prompt and generate draft.
  * Validate prompt entry; handle API errors gracefully.
  * Show generated draft in main editor.
* **Step 3:** Trigger NotebookLM validation.
  * One-click validation sends content to third-party NotebookLM library.
  * Return and display validation score/flags and doctrinal citations.
  * Validation panel anchored on the right side of the editor.
* **Step 4:** Content curation and editing.
  * Edit draft based on validation results.
  * Option to view citation sources directly.
  * Save drafts and track validation history.
* **Step 5:** Assign to channel and schedule.
  * Tag content to delivery platform.
  * Choose publication date via editorial calendar UI.
  * Status updates to “Scheduled.”
* **Step 6:** Mark as published and archive.
  * Mark content as published after delivery.
  * Optionally archive for future reference and analytics.

**Advanced Features & Edge Cases**

* Bulk scheduling: Assign dates and channels for multiple items at once.
* Validation history: View prior validation runs and responses.
* Content versioning: Track major edits and revert if necessary.
* Handle validation or AI API downtime gracefully (clear errors, retry options).

**UI/UX Highlights**

* Single-column editor central to workflow; validation feedback panel fixed to the right.
* Editorial calendar as a visual week/month view; click-in to schedule or review items.
* Portuguese-first interface and localization for SEO/blog workflows.
* Consistent, accessible color palette and responsive design for all device types.

---

## Narrative

Ramir sits down at his desk on a Tuesday morning with inspiration to create a 1-minute video script about the upcoming Feast of Corpus Christi. He opens his Catholic Content Management Platform and immediately sees his dashboard, which highlights content drafts and this week’s publishing schedule. Selecting the “short video” template, he enters a brief prompt for Claude, who quickly generates a concise, engaging script in less than a minute.

Before moving forward, Ramir clicks the “Validate with NotebookLM” button. Within seconds, the platform provides a score indicating strong doctrinal support but flags two statements for clarification. The tool seamlessly surfaces source citations from the Catechism and Vatican II documents, making it easy for Ramir to reference and refine his wording to ensure precise Catholic teaching.

Satisfied with the script, he assigns it to his short-video channel and schedules it for Thursday’s publication. Motivated by the smooth workflow, he turns to the blog post section—writes, validates, optimizes SEO in Portuguese, and schedules another article, all in one productive sitting. Ramir knows that every piece he publishes aligns with Church authority, publishes on time, and is organized for maximum reach—giving him both confidence and creative momentum.

---

## Success Metrics

### User-Centric Metrics

* Average time (in minutes) from content idea to scheduled item (tracked via timestamps).
* Number of validation cycles per content piece before approval.
* Number of content pieces published per week.
* User feedback on validation accuracy and workflow satisfaction.

### Business Metrics

* Consistency of weekly publishing cadence (minimum 3 items/week).
* Reduction in after-publication corrections or removals due to doctrinal issues.
* Adoption/continued use rate (e.g., items managed per week/month).

### Technical Metrics

* Average response time from NotebookLM API (<2 seconds per call).
* Average AI generation latency (<5 seconds per request).
* Content editor uptime (>99% during weekday business hours).
* Robust error-rate tracking on API integrations (<2% failure rate).

### Tracking Plan

* Content item created (event)
* AI generation triggered (event, with selected model)
* Validation initiated/completed (event, with score/result)
* Content scheduled (event, with channel/date)
* Marked as published (event)
* User edits/re-validation cycles (event)

---

## Technical Considerations

### Technical Needs

* Web application frontend with content editor, dashboard, and calendar views.
* API integrations for Anthropic (Claude), OpenAI (ChatGPT), and third-party NotebookLM library.
* Lightweight backend for routing requests, managing validation/scheduling logic, and storing user data.
* Database for persistent storage of content items, validation responses, scheduling metadata, and channel assignments.

### Integration Points

* Anthropic Claude API for content drafting.
* OpenAI ChatGPT API for alternate drafting option.
* NotebookLM third-party library for source validation and citation retrieval.
* Option for editorial calendar export (iCal or Google Calendar integration for reminders).

### Data Storage & Privacy

* All data (drafts, validation results, scheduling info) stored privately and securely for Ramir only.
* Ensure encryption at rest and in transit.
* No personal data shared externally or sold.
* Compliance with GDPR and local privacy standards as relevant.

### Scalability & Performance

* Designed for single-user, low daily volume (tens of content items per week).
* Minimal scaling risk.
* Prioritize fast response for workflow efficiency.

### Potential Challenges

* Reliability and rate limits of NotebookLM’s third-party library/API.
* Maintaining consistent, high-quality Portuguese output from AI models (especially for SEO).
* Managing failing API calls or downtime from AI or validation services.
* Ensuring third-party library con

Ramir sits down at his desk on a Tuesday morning with inspiration to create a 1-minute video script about the upcoming Feast of Corpus Christi. He opens his Catholic Content Management Platform and immediately sees his dashboard, which highlights content drafts and this week’s publishing schedule. Selecting the “short video” template, he enters a brief prompt for Claude, who quickly generates a concise, engaging script in less than a minute.

Before moving forward, Ramir clicks the “Validate with NotebookLM” button. Within seconds, the platform provides a score indicating strong doctrinal support but flags two statements for clarification. The tool seamlessly surfaces source citations from the Catechism and Vatican II documents, making it easy for Ramir to reference and refine his wording to ensure precise Catholic teaching.

Satisfied with the script, he assigns it to his short-video channel and schedules it for Thursday’s publication. Motivated by the smooth workflow, he turns to the blog post section—writes, validates, optimizes SEO in Portuguese, and schedules another article, all in one productive sitting. Ramir knows that every piece he publishes aligns with Church authority, publishes on time, and is organized for maximum reach—giving him both confidence and creative momentum.

---

## Success Metrics

### User-Centric Metrics

* Average time (in minutes) from content idea to scheduled item (tracked via timestamps).
* Number of validation cycles per content piece before approval.
* Number of content pieces published per week.
* User feedback on validation accuracy and workflow satisfaction.

### Business Metrics

* Consistency of weekly publishing cadence (minimum 3 items/week).
* Reduction in after-publication corrections or removals due to doctrinal issues.
* Adoption/continued use rate (e.g., items managed per week/month).

### Technical Metrics

* Average response time from NotebookLM API (<2 seconds per call).
* Average AI generation latency (<5 seconds per request).
* Content editor uptime (>99% during weekday business hours).
* Robust error-rate tracking on API integrations (<2% failure rate).

### Tracking Plan

* Content item created (event)
* AI generation triggered (event, with selected model)
* Validation initiated/completed (event, with score/result)
* Content scheduled (event, with channel/date)
* Marked as published (event)
* User edits/re-validation cycles (event)

---

## Technical Considerations

### Technical Needs

* Web application frontend with content editor, dashboard, and calendar views.
* API integrations for Anthropic (Claude), OpenAI (ChatGPT), and third-party NotebookLM library.
* Lightweight backend for routing requests, managing validation/scheduling logic, and storing user data.
* Database for persistent storage of content items, validation responses, scheduling metadata, and channel assignments.

### Integration Points

* Anthropic Claude API for content drafting.
* OpenAI ChatGPT API for alternate drafting option.
* NotebookLM third-party library for source validation and citation retrieval.
* Option for editorial calendar export (iCal or Google Calendar integration for reminders).

### Data Storage & Privacy

* All data (drafts, validation results, scheduling info) stored privately and securely for Ramir only.
* Ensure encryption at rest and in transit.
* No personal data shared externally or sold.
* Compliance with GDPR and local privacy standards as relevant.

### Scalability & Performance

* Designed for single-user, low daily volume (tens of content items per week).
* Minimal scaling risk.
* Prioritize fast response for workflow efficiency.

### Potential Challenges

* Reliability and rate limits of NotebookLM’s third-party library/API.
* Maintaining consistent, high-quality Portuguese output from AI models (especially for SEO).
* Managing failing API calls or downtime from AI or validation services.\

1	{
2	  "task_description": "# Catholic Content Management Platform – PRD

### TL;DR

A personal platform for Ramir to manage the full lifecycle of Catholic content — from AI-assisted generation (Claude, ChatGPT) to theological validation via NotebookLM, editorial scheduling, and multi-channel delivery. It specifically supports short-form video scripts (1-min), long-form video scripts (10-min), and SEO-optimized blog posts in Portuguese, ensuring both speed and doctrinal accuracy for a Catholic content creator.

---

## Goals

### Business Goals

* Centralize all Catholic content production activities in a single platform.
* Reduce average time-to-publish for each piece of content by at least 50%.
* Increase consistency in publishing, achieving a minimum of 3 content pieces per week.
* Ensure 100% of published content is validated for theological accuracy.
* Minimize post-publication corrections by implementing robust doctrinal review.

### User Goals

* Rapidly generate content using AI while maintaining a uniquely Catholic voice.
* Validate content against authoritative Church sources (without building custom RAG pipelines).
* Organize content into an accessible editorial calendar with clear status tracking.
* Schedule delivery for each piece on appropriate channels (e.g., YouTube, Blog).
* Easily curate, update, and archive content with full version and validation history.

### Non-Goals

* Building or maintaining a custom Catholic knowledge base (handled entirely by NotebookLM).
* Undertaking broad social media management (e.g., analytics, community interaction) beyond basic scheduling/delivery reminders.
* Implementing monetization or revenue-generating features.

---

## User Stories

**Persona:** Ramir, Catholic Content Creator

* As Ramir, I want to create a new short-form video script using Claude or ChatGPT, so that I can generate engaging 1-min video content quickly.
* As Ramir, I want to validate my script against the full Catholic corpus available in NotebookLM, so that I am confident my content aligns with Church teaching.
* As Ramir, I want to schedule my validated content on an editorial calendar, so that I maintain a consistent publishing cadence across channels.
* As Ramir, I want to curate and edit blog posts for SEO (in Portuguese), so that my written content reaches a larger audience via search engines.
* As Ramir, I want to review the current status (draft, validated, scheduled, published) and destination (YouTube, Blog, etc.) of all my content, so that I’m always aware of what’s next.

---

## Functional Requirements

* **Content Creation (Priority: High)**

  * AI Drafting Integration: Connect to Claude and ChatGPT APIs for rapid content generation.
  * Content Type Templates: Pre-configured templates for short-form video, long-form video, and blog posts.

* **Theological Validation (Priority: High)**

  * NotebookLM Integration: Use a third-party library for doctrinal source validation.
  * Validation Score/Flag System: Visual feedback indicating theological alignment.
  * Source Citation Display: Display relevant citations from the Catholic corpus.

* **Editorial Calendar (Priority: Medium)**

  * Scheduling by Content Type and Channel: Assign publication dates and platforms.
  * Status Tracking: Transparently manage content states (draft, validated, scheduled, published).

* **SEO & Curation Tools (Priority: Medium)**

  * Portuguese SEO Metadata: Fields and checks for keywords, meta, and descriptions in Portuguese.
  * Readability Checks: Automated assessment of text clarity.
  * Keyword Suggestions: AI-powered prompts for blog post optimization.

* **Channel Management (Priority: Low)**

  * Channel Tagging: Associate content with YouTube, Blog, or other platforms.
  * Delivery Reminders: Notify when content is due to be published or reviewed.

---

## User Experience

**Entry Point & First-Time User Experience**

* User logs in and lands on a dashboard summarizing the content pipeline, showing draft/validated/scheduled/published items, and the editorial calendar.
* Optional onboarding walkthrough highlights how to create content, trigger validation, and use the editor.

**Core Experience**

* **Step 1:** Create a new content item.
  * Choose content type: short video, long video, or blog post.
  * Select language preference (default: Portuguese for blog).
  * Clear, minimal form – no unnecessary friction.
* **Step 2:** AI generation panel.
  * Choose AI model: Claude or ChatGPT.
  * Enter topic/prompt and generate draft.
  * Validate prompt entry; handle API errors gracefully.
  * Show generated draft in main editor.
* **Step 3:** Trigger NotebookLM validation.
  * One-click validation sends content to third-party NotebookLM library.
  * Return and display validation score/flags and doctrinal citations.
  * Validation panel anchored on the right side of the editor.
* **Step 4:** Content curation and editing.
  * Edit draft based on validation results.
  * Option to view citation sources directly.
  * Save drafts and track validation history.
* **Step 5:** Assign to channel and schedule.
  * Tag content to delivery platform.
  * Choose publication date via editorial calendar UI.
  * Status updates to “Scheduled.”
* **Step 6:** Mark as published and archive.
  * Mark content as published after delivery.
  * Optionally archive for future reference and analytics.

**Advanced Features & Edge Cases**

* Bulk scheduling: Assign dates and channels for multiple items at once.
* Validation history: View prior validation runs and responses.
* Content versioning: Track major edits and revert if necessary.
* Handle validation or AI API downtime gracefully (clear errors, retry options).

**UI/UX Highlights**

* Single-column editor central to workflow; validation feedback panel fixed to the right.
* Editorial calendar as a visual week/month view; click-in to schedule or review items.
* Portuguese-first interface and localization for SEO/blog workflows.
* Consistent, accessible color palette and responsive design for all device types.

---

## Narrative

Ramir sits down at his desk on a Tuesday morning with inspiration to create a 1-minute video script about the upcoming Feast of Corpus Christi. He opens his Catholic Content Management Platform and immediately sees his dashboard, which highlights content drafts and this week’s publishing schedule. Selecting the “short video” template, he enters a brief prompt for Claude, who quickly generates a concise, engaging script in less than a minute.

Before moving forward, Ramir clicks the “Validate with NotebookLM” button. Within seconds, the platform provides a score indicating strong doctrinal support but flags two statements for clarification. The tool seamlessly surfaces source citations from the Catechism and Vatican II documents, making it easy for Ramir to reference and refine his wording to ensure precise Catholic teaching.

Satisfied with the script, he assigns it to his short-video channel and schedules it for Thursday’s publication. Motivated by the smooth workflow, he turns to the blog post section—writes, validates, optimizes SEO in Portuguese, and schedules another article, all in one productive sitting. Ramir knows that every piece he publishes aligns with Church authority, publishes on time, and is organized for maximum reach—giving him both confidence and creative momentum.

---

## Success Metrics

### User-Centric Metrics

* Average time (in minutes) from content idea to scheduled item (tracked via timestamps).
* Number of validation cycles per content piece before approval.
* Number of content pieces published per week.
* User feedback on validation accuracy and workflow satisfaction.

### Business Metrics

* Consistency of weekly publishing cadence (minimum 3 items/week).
* Reduction in after-publication corrections or removals due to doctrinal issues.
* Adoption/continued use rate (e.g., items managed per week/month).

### Technical Metrics

* Average response time from NotebookLM API (<2 seconds per call).
* Average AI generation latency (<5 seconds per request).
* Content editor uptime (>99% during weekday business hours).
* Robust error-rate tracking on API integrations (<2% failure rate).

### Tracking Plan

* Content item created (event)
* AI generation triggered (event, with selected model)
* Validation initiated/completed (event, with score/result)
* Content scheduled (event, with channel/date)
* Marked as published (event)
* User edits/re-validation cycles (event)

---

## Technical Considerations

### Technical Needs

* Web application frontend with content editor, dashboard, and calendar views.
* API integrations for Anthropic (Claude), OpenAI (ChatGPT), and third-party NotebookLM library.
* Lightweight backend for routing requests, managing validation/scheduling logic, and storing user data.
* Database for persistent storage of content items, validation responses, scheduling metadata, and channel assignments.

### Integration Points

* Anthropic Claude API for content drafting.
* OpenAI ChatGPT API for alternate drafting option.
* NotebookLM third-party library for source validation and citation retrieval.
* Option for editorial calendar export (iCal or Google Calendar integration for reminders).

### Data Storage & Privacy

* All data (drafts, validation results, scheduling info) stored privately and securely for Ramir only.
* Ensure encryption at rest and in transit.
* No personal data shared externally or sold.
* Compliance with GDPR and local privacy standards as relevant.

### Scalability & Performance

* Designed for single-user, low daily volume (tens of content items per week).
* Minimal scaling risk.
* Prioritize fast response for workflow efficiency.

### Potential Challenges

* Reliability and rate limits of NotebookLM’s third-party library/API.
* Maintaining consistent, high-quality Portuguese output from AI models (especially for SEO).
* Managing failing API calls or downtime from AI or validation services.
* Ensuring third-party library con

entries.detail:
1	# Specification: Verbum — Catholic Content Management Platform
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	Verbum is a greenfield, single-user Catholic content management platform for Ramir, a Portuguese-speaking Catholic content creator. The platform centralizes the full content lifecycle — from AI-assisted generation (via Anthropic Claude and OpenAI ChatGPT) through theological validation (via NotebookLM), editorial scheduling, and multi-channel delivery. It supports three content types: short-form video scripts (1 min), long-form video scripts (10 min), and SEO-optimized blog posts in Portuguese. The goal is to reduce time-to-publish by 50%, ensure 100% doctrinal accuracy through automated validation, and maintain a consistent publishing cadence of at least 3 pieces per week. The entire system is designed for a single user — no multi-tenancy, no scaling concerns, no monetization.
6	
7	## Workflow Type
8	
9	**Type**: feature
10	
11	**Rationale**: This is a brand-new full-stack application being built from scratch within an empty repository. There is no existing application logic to refactor or migrate — only a Vite/React/Tailwind prototype shell (`prototypes-magic/`) with reusable UI components. Every layer (database schema, backend API, service integrations, frontend pages) must be designed and implemented.
12	
13	## Task Scope
14	
15	### Services Involved
16	- **verbum-backend** (primary) — Python FastAPI backend handling API routing, AI integration orchestration, NotebookLM validation, database persistence, and scheduling logic
17	- **verbum-frontend** (primary) — React TypeScript SPA providing the dashboard, content editor, validation panel, editorial calendar, and SEO tools
18	- **SQLite database** (infrastructure) — Embedded file-based database for content items, validation records, scheduling metadata, and version history
19	
20	### This Task Will:
21	- [ ] Set up the full project structure (backend + frontend monorepo)
22	- [ ] Implement the Python FastAPI backend with async architecture
23	- [ ] Design and create the SQLite database schema via SQLModel
24	- [ ] Integrate Anthropic Claude API for AI content generation with streaming
25	- [ ] Integrate OpenAI ChatGPT API as an alternate drafting engine with streaming
26	- [ ] Integrate NotebookLM (via `notebooklm-py`) for theological validation with citation retrieval
27	- [ ] Build a validation abstraction layer to enable graceful degradation and provider swapping
28	- [ ] Create the React frontend with dashboard, content editor, validation panel, and calendar views
29	- [ ] Implement the full content lifecycle: draft → validated → scheduled → published
30	- [ ] Add Portuguese SEO metadata tools for blog post optimization
31	- [ ] Implement content versioning and validation history tracking
32	- [ ] Add iCal export for editorial calendar reminders
33	- [ ] Implement environment-based configuration with secure API key management
34	
35	### Out of Scope:
36	- Custom Catholic knowledge base or RAG pipeline (NotebookLM handles this)
37	- Social media analytics or community management
38	- Monetization or revenue features
39	- Multi-user authentication or multi-tenancy
40	- Direct Google Calendar API integration (using .ics export instead)
41	- Bulk social media publishing automation
42	- Mobile native app (responsive web only)
43	- Docker containerization (single-user local/hosted deployment)
44	
45	## Service Context
46	
47	### verbum-backend
48	
49	**Tech Stack:**
50	- Language: Python 3.11+
51	- Framework: FastAPI >= 0.135.0 (with `[standard]` extras for uvicorn)
52	- ORM: SQLModel >= 0.0.37 (Pydantic + SQLAlchemy combined)
53	- Database: SQLite (via aiosqlite for async operations)
54	- AI SDKs: `anthropic >= 0.88.0`, `openai >= 2.30.0`
55	- Validation: `notebooklm-py[browser] >= 0.3.4` (UNOFFICIAL — HIGH RISK)
56	- Calendar: `icalendar` for .ics export
57	- Config: `python-dotenv` for environment variable management
58	- Key directories: `backend/app/`, `backend/app/api/`, `backend/app/models/`, `backend/app/services/`, `backend/app/core/`

204	## Patterns to Follow
205	
206	### Pattern 1: Async FastAPI with SQLModel
207	
208	Industry best practice for Python async web applications:
209	
210	```python
211	# backend/app/main.py
212	from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
213	from fastapi import FastAPI
214	from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware
215	from app.core.database import init_db
216	from app.api import content, generation, validation, schedule, dashboard
217	
218	@asynccontextmanager
219	async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
220	    await init_db()
221	    yield
222	
223	app = FastAPI(title="Verbum API", lifespan=lifespan)
224	app.add_middleware(
225	    CORSMiddleware,
226	    allow_origins=["http://localhost:5173"],
227	    allow_credentials=True,
228	    allow_methods=["*"],
229	    allow_headers=["*"],
230	)
231	app.include_router(content.router, prefix="/api/content", tags=["content"])
232	app.include_router(generation.router, prefix="/api/generation", tags=["generation"])
233	app.include_router(validation.router, prefix="/api/validation", tags=["validation"])
234	app.include_router(schedule.router, prefix="/api/schedule", tags=["schedule"])
235	app.include_router(dashboard.router, prefix="/api/dashboard", tags=["dashboard"])
236	```
237	
238	**Key Points:**
239	- Use `lifespan` context manager for database initialization (not deprecated `@app.on_event`)
240	- Configure CORS for frontend-backend communication
241	- Organize endpoints into routers with prefixes and tags
242	- All handlers should be `async def` for non-blocking I/O
243	
244	### Pattern 2: SQLModel Data Models with Status Enum
245	
246	```python
247	# backend/app/models/content.py
248	from enum import Enum
249	from datetime import datetime
250	from typing import Optional
251	from sqlmodel import SQLModel, Field
252	from uuid import uuid4
253	
254	class ContentType(str, Enum):
255	    SHORT_VIDEO = "short_video"
256	    LONG_VIDEO = "long_video"
257	    BLOG_POST = "blog_post"
258	
259	class ContentStatus(str, Enum):
260	    DRAFT = "draft"
261	    VALIDATED = "validated"
262	    SCHEDULED = "scheduled"
263	    PUBLISHED = "published"
264	
265	class ContentItem(SQLModel, table=True):
266	    __tablename__ = "content_items"
267	
268	    id: str = Field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid4()), primary_key=True)
269	    title: str
270	    body: str = ""
271	    content_type: ContentType
272	    status: ContentStatus = ContentStatus.DRAFT
273	    language: str = "pt"
274	    channel: Optional[str] = None
275	    ai_model_used: Optional[str] = None
276	    scheduled_date: Optional[datetime] = None
277	    created_at: datetime = Field(default_factory=datetime.utcnow)
278	    updated_at: datetime = Field(default_factory=datetime.utcnow)
279	    version: int = 1
280	```
281	
282	**Key Points:**
283	- Use string enums for readable database values
284	- UUID primary keys for content items
285	- Default to Portuguese language (`pt`)
286	- Track which AI model generated the draft
287	- Version counter for content versioning

289	### Pattern 3: AI Service Abstraction with Streaming
290	
291	```python
292	# backend/app/services/ai_service.py
293	from typing import Protocol, AsyncIterator
294	
295	class AIProvider(Protocol):
296	    async def generate(self, prompt: str, system_prompt: str, max_tokens: int) -> str: ...
297	    async def generate_stream(self, prompt: str, system_prompt: str, max_tokens: int) -> AsyncIterator[str]: ...
298	
299	class AIService:
300	    def __init__(self, providers: dict[str, AIProvider]):
301	        self._providers = providers
302	
303	    async def generate(self, model_key: str, prompt: str, system_prompt: str, max_tokens: int = 2048) -> str:
304	        provider = self._providers[model_key]
305	        return await provider.generate(prompt, system_prompt, max_tokens)
306	
307	    async def generate_stream(self, model_key: str, prompt: str, system_prompt: str, max_tokens: int = 2048) -> AsyncIterator[str]:
308	        provider = self._providers[model_key]
309	        async for chunk in provider.generate_stream(prompt, system_prompt, max_tokens):
310	            yield chunk
311	```
312	
313	**Key Points:**
314	- Use Python `Protocol` for interface definition (structural typing)
315	- Support both full-response and streaming generation
316	- Provider pattern allows easy addition of new AI models
317	- The `model_key` selects between Claude and ChatGPT
318	
319	### Pattern 4: Validation Service with Fallback
320	
321	```python
322	# backend/app/services/validation_service.py
323	from typing import Protocol
324	from dataclasses import dataclass
325	
326	@dataclass
327	class Citation:
328	    citation_number: int
329	    source_id: str
330	    cited_text: str
331	
332	@dataclass
333	class ValidationResult:
334	    score: float  # 0.0 to 1.0
335	    flags: list[str]
336	    citations: list[Citation]
337	    provider: str  # "notebooklm" or "ai_fallback"
338	    raw_answer: str
339	
340	class ValidationProvider(Protocol):
341	    async def validate(self, content: str, content_type: str) -> ValidationResult: ...
342	    async def is_available(self) -> bool: ...
343	
344	class ValidationService:
345	    def __init__(self, primary: ValidationProvider, fallback: ValidationProvider):
346	        self._primary = primary
347	        self._fallback = fallback
348	
349	    async def validate(self, content: str, content_type: str) -> ValidationResult:
350	        if await self._primary.is_available():
351	            try:
352	                return await self._primary.validate(content, content_type)
353	            except Exception:
354	                pass
355	        return await self._fallback.validate(content, content_type)
356	```
357	
358	**Key Points:**
359	- Abstraction layer critical for NotebookLM reliability concerns
360	- `is_available()` check before attempting validation
361	- Automatic fallback to AI-based validation if NotebookLM is down
362	- Structured `ValidationResult` with score, flags, and traceable citations
363	- The `provider` field tells the UI which backend produced the result
364	
365	### Pattern 5: SSE Streaming from FastAPI to React
366	
367	```python
368	# backend/app/api/generation.py
369	from fastapi import APIRouter
370	from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
371	
372	router = APIRouter()
373	
374	@router.post("/stream")
375	async def generate_stream(request: GenerationRequest):
376	    async def event_generator():
377	        async for chunk in ai_service.generate_stream(
378	            model_key=request.model,
379	            prompt=request.prompt,
380	            system_prompt=request.system_prompt,
381	        ):
382	            yield f"data: {json.dumps({'text': chunk})}\n\n"
383	        yield f"data: {json.dumps({'done': True})}\n\n"
384	
385	    return StreamingResponse(event_generator(), media_type="text/event-stream")
386	```
387	
388	```typescript
389	// frontend/src/services/streaming.ts
390	export async function* streamGeneration(request: GenerationRequest): AsyncGenerator<string> {
391	  const response = await fetch('/api/generation/stream', {
392	    method: 'POST',
393	    headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
394	    body: JSON.stringify(request),
395	  });
396	  const reader = response.body!.getReader();
397	  const decoder = new TextDecoder();
398	  while (true) {
399	    const { done, value } = await reader.read();

400 if (done) break; 401 const text = decoder.decode(value); 402 for (const line of text.split('\n')) { 403 if (line.startsWith('data: ')) { 404 const data = JSON.parse(line.slice(6)); 405 if (data.done) return; 406 yield data.text; 407 } 408 } 409 } 410 } 411 ``` 412 413 **Key Points:** 414 - FastAPI `StreamingResponse` with SSE format (`data: ...\n\n`) 415 - React consumes via `ReadableStream` reader 416 - JSON-encoded chunks allow metadata alongside text 417 - Terminal `done` event signals completion 418 - Dramatically improves perceived performance for content generation 419 420 ### Pattern 6: React Page with Editor + Right Panel Layout 421 422 ```tsx 423 // frontend/src/pages/EditorPage.tsx 424 export function EditorPage() { 425 return ( 426
427 {/* Main Editor - Single Column */} 428
429 430 431 432
433 {/* Validation Panel - Fixed Right */} 434
435 436
437
438 ); 439 } 440 ``` 441 442 **Key Points:** 443 - Flexbox layout with fixed-width right panel (per UX requirements) 444 - Editor takes remaining space (`flex-1`) 445 - Validation panel is always visible alongside editor 446 - Both panels independently scrollable 447 448 ## Requirements 449 450 ### Functional Requirements 451 452 1. **AI Content Generation (High Priority)** 453 - Description: Connect to Anthropic Claude and OpenAI ChatGPT APIs to generate content drafts from user prompts. Support three content type templates: short-form video (1 min), long-form video (10 min), and SEO blog post. Stream AI responses in real-time to the editor. 454 - Acceptance: User can select an AI model (Claude or ChatGPT), enter a prompt, and see the generated draft stream into the editor in under 5 seconds. Content type template structures the system prompt appropriately. 455 456 2. **Content Type Templates (High Priority)** 457 - Description: Pre-configured templates that structure AI prompts and editor layouts for each content type. Short video templates target 150-200 words; long video templates target 1500-2000 words; blog post templates include SEO fields. 458 - Acceptance: Each content type produces appropriately structured output with correct length targets and format (script format for videos, article format for blogs). 459 460 3. **Theological Validation via NotebookLM (High Priority)** 461 - Description: One-click validation sends content to NotebookLM for doctrinal review against loaded Catholic sources. Returns a validation score (0-100%), flag list for issues, and source citations from the Catholic corpus (Catechism, Vatican II, etc.). 462 - Acceptance: Validation completes in under 2 seconds (target), displays score + flags + citations in the right panel. If NotebookLM is unavailable, falls back to AI-based validation with a clear indicator. 463 464 4. **Source Citation Display (High Priority)** 465 - Description: Display traceable citations from NotebookLM validation, showing the specific text passages from Catholic sources that support or contradict the content. 466 - Acceptance: Each citation shows the citation number, source name, and exact quoted text. Citations are expandable/collapsible. The user can see which parts of their content are supported by which sources. 467 468 5. **Content Lifecycle Status Tracking (Medium Priority)** 469 - Description: Each content item moves through a linear status pipeline: draft → validated → scheduled → published. Status transitions are tracked with timestamps. Dashboard shows counts per status. 470 - Acceptance: Status is visible on all content views (list, editor, calendar). Only valid transitions are allowed (e.g., cannot schedule without validation). Dashboard shows accurate pipeline counts. 471 472 6. **Editorial Calendar (Medium Priority)** 473 - Description: Visual week/month calendar view showing scheduled content items. Click to schedule, reschedule, or view content details. Supports filtering by content type and channel. 474 - Acceptance: Calendar displays all scheduled items on their publication dates. User can drag/click to schedule items. Week and month views both work. Items show content type icon, title, and channel. 475 476 7. **Portuguese SEO Metadata (Medium Priority)** 477 - Description: For blog posts, provide fields for Portuguese meta title, meta description, keywords, and slug. Include automated readability assessment and AI-powered keyword suggestions in Portuguese. 478 - Acceptance: SEO panel shows all metadata fields when editing a blog post. Readability score is calculated automatically. Keyword suggestions can be triggered via AI. 479 480 8. **Channel Management (Low Priority)** 481 - Description: Tag content with delivery channels (YouTube, Blog, other). Each content item can be assigned to one or more channels. Channels appear on calendar and list views. 482 - Acceptance: User can tag content with channels, filter by channel, and see channel assignments on calendar. 483 484 9. **iCal Export (Low Priority)** 485 - Description: Export the editorial calendar as an .ics file compatible with Google Calendar, Apple Calendar, and Outlook. 486 - Acceptance: Exported .ics file contains all scheduled items with correct dates, titles, and channel info. File imports successfully into Google Calendar. 487 488 10. **Content Versioning (Medium Priority)** 489 - Description: Track version history for each content item. Each save after initial creation increments the version. Users can view previous versions and their associated validation results. 490 - Acceptance: Version history is accessible from the editor. Each version shows a timestamp and can be viewed (read-only). Validation results are linked to the version they were run against. 491 492 ### Edge Cases 493 494 1. **NotebookLM API Unavailable** — Fall back to AI-based validation (Claude/GPT with loaded Catholic source context). Display clear indicator that fallback provider was used, with lower confidence disclaimer. 495 2. **AI Generation Timeout** — If streaming doesn't start within 10 seconds, show timeout error with retry button. Partial streamed content is preserved in the editor. 496 3. **AI Generation API Key Missing** — Show setup instructions in the generation panel instead of the model selector. Do not expose which key is missing in the UI. 497 4. **Concurrent Validation + Generation** — Allow user to trigger validation while a generation is still streaming. Both operations should run independently. 498 5. **Empty Content Validation** — Prevent validation trigger when content body is empty. Show inline hint. 499 6. **Invalid Status Transition** — Prevent scheduling unvalidated content. Show toast notification explaining the required workflow step. 500 7. **Large Content Exceeding Token Limits** — For very long blog posts, chunk the content for validation. For generation, warn user if prompt + expected output exceeds model context window. 501 8. **NotebookLM Auth Token Expiry** — Handle token refresh automatically. If refresh fails, show re-authentication instructions. 502 9. **Calendar Date in the Past** — Allow scheduling in the past (for backdating published content) but show a warning. 503 10. **Portuguese Character Encoding** — Ensure UTF-8 encoding throughout the stack for proper Portuguese diacritical marks (ã, õ, ç, é, etc.). 504 505 ## Implementation Notes 506 507 ### DO 508 - Use `async def` for ALL FastAPI endpoints and service methods — the entire backend must be non-blocking 509 - Use Python `Protocol` classes for AI and validation provider interfaces — enables clean dependency injection and testing 510 - Reuse UI components from `prototypes-magic/src/components/ui/` — copy them into `frontend/src/components/ui/` 511 - Use `class-variance-authority` (CVA) for component variants — it's already in the prototype dependencies 512 - Use SQLModel's combined Pydantic+SQLAlchemy models — avoids duplicate model/schema definitions 513 - Implement streaming via SSE for all AI generation — dramatically improves UX 514 - Wrap ALL external API calls in try/except with specific error types (`anthropic.APIError`, OpenAI exceptions, `RPCError`) 515 - Use `python-dotenv` and Pydantic `BaseSettings` for configuration — validates env vars at startup 516 - Include `model` parameter in every Anthropic API call — there is no default model 517 - Create one client instance per provider and reuse — don't create per-request clients 518 - Default to Portuguese (`pt`) for all content and SEO operations 519 - Use UUID strings for all primary keys — enables offline-friendly ID generation 520 521 ### DON'T 522 - Don't create a custom Catholic knowledge base or RAG pipeline — NotebookLM handles this 523 - Don't use the OpenAI Responses API — use Chat Completions API for stability 524 - Don't use synchronous database operations — always use `AsyncSession` with `aiosqlite` 525 - Don't expose API keys in frontend code — all AI calls go through the backend 526 - Don't use `@app.on_event("startup")` — use the `lifespan` context manager (deprecated in modern FastAPI) 527 - Don't hardcode AI model names — make them configurable via environment variables with sensible defaults 528 - Don't build multi-user auth — this is a single-user personal tool 529 - Don't use Playwright/Chromium in production for NotebookLM auth — use `NOTEBOOKLM_AUTH_JSON` env var with pre-captured tokens 530 - Don't block the event loop with `time.sleep()` or synchronous HTTP calls — use `asyncio.sleep()` and async clients 531 532 ## Data Model 533 534 ### Core Tables 535 536 ``` 537 content_items 538 ├── id: UUID (PK) 539 ├── title: str 540 ├── body: text 541 ├── content_type: enum (short_video | long_video | blog_post) 542 ├── status: enum (draft | validated | scheduled | published) 543 ├── language: str (default: "pt") 544 ├── channel: str (nullable) — "youtube", "blog", et ...
